EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the mid-Atlantic on the morning of June 3. Considerable cloud cover lingered during and after the front passage, but breaks of sunshine gradually developed by afternoon. This, combined with a surge of moisture behind the warm front, allowed a moderate amount of instability to develop. A pre-frontal surface trough served as a focus for convective development by late afternoon. Convection became fairly widespread, but the moderate instability combined with only weak to moderate shear limited its overall severity, and the high moisture contributed to a messy convective evolution. A few reports of damaging wind were received, and storms also produced locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed near Somers Point Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Ida impacted the mid-Atlantic on September 1, 2021. As it moved northward, Ida began transitioning to a strong mid-latitude cyclone with pronounced frontal features, but with continued tropical moisture. On a regional level, the remnants of Ida became one of the most severe natural disasters in US history, due to a combination of several tornadoes, some of them strong, as well as catastrophic flooding over a large and densely populated area. The severe weather threat unfolded as the system's developing warm front lifted northward from the Delmarva region into Pennsylvania and New Jersey. South of the warm front, partial clearing and moderate to strong instability developed. Meanwhile, very strong shear and forcing, along with tropical moisture, were present within the warm sector. The result was widespread convection in the form of both supercell and quasi-linear thunderstorms. A number of storms became severe across the region, producing several tornadoes and other instances of wind damage. This continued an exceptionally active stretch of high end severe weather in the region, coming barely a month after the tornado outbreak of July 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down along Emmons Drive along Route 1 in |Princeton, NJ as a remnant supercell moved northward after |producing the Mullica Hill and Burlington, NJ tornadoes earlier |Wednesday afternoon as Ida's remnants moved through the region. |Damage first occurred as several small branches were brought down |along the Whole Foods and Wendy's parking lots along Route 1 in |far eastern Princeton Township, with a continued path of minor |branch damage extending northward along Farber Road, Meadow Drive,|and Canal Pointe Boulevard adjacent to Route 1. Several medium-|size trees were also uprooted with numerous small branches having |been brought down along the western periphery of the Marketfair |shopping parking lot. ||From there, the tornado continued northward with minor tree |damage having occurred along Carnegie Center Boulevard as the |tornado moved northward. Several small branches were also brought |down in a residential area along Heritage Boulevard with one pine |tree even having been snapped. An additional pine tree was also |uprooted along Alexander Road near Glenview. The tornado then |snapped several small hardwood trees, knocking down several |utility poles and power lines along Washington Road. A tree was |also uprooted along Lower Harrison Street along Carnegie Lake |before the tornado lifted somewhere over the lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward moving cold front stalled across northern New Jersey and northeast Pennsylvania early on September 13. Warm and humid conditions were present south of the front. An upper level disturbance moving through the westerly flow aloft helped to increase wind shear. Combined with the instability, this disturbance caused a line of thunderstorms to move through the region during the evening hours of September 13. This line of storms became severe, with a number of reports of damaging wind along it. The line moved offshore and out of the region later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pole and wires were downed on US-46 eastbound in the area of Mount Olive Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Post Tropical Cyclone Ida brought heavy rain to New Jersey on September 1.  Rainfall totals were as high as 5 to 10 inches.  The heavy rain caused significant flash flooding, mainly in the northern half of the state.  It resulted in widespread property damage.  There were several fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NJ Route 73 in Palmyra was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa approached the mid-Atlantic states from the south on July 8. Downstream of Elsa, a warm and moist environment was present. A slow moving frontal boundary also resided over northern New Jersey, northern Pennsylvania, and into New York. This frontal system provided forcing for convective development over the mid-Atlantic, in fairly close proximity but unrelated to Tropical Storm Elsa. With strong instability and weak to moderate wind shear, some of the storms became severe as they organized into linear and multi-cellular clusters. Deep layer southerly flow in advance of Elsa caused storms to move northward as opposed to a more typical eastward motion. Damaging wind was the main hazard associated with these storms, with several strong downbursts noted. Storm activity eventually weakened and moved north of the region by evening as Elsa moved in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were snapped and patio furniture was overturned. A personal weather station measured gusts over 50 mph. Based on a video provided of the incident, actual wind speeds were likely higher. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough moved east-southeastward out of the Great Lakes region on July 29. An associated, strengthening surface low also tracked out of the Great Lakes and into upstate New York. A warm front associated with this low moved through most of New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania during the late morning and early afternoon hours. This front, combined with the remains of widespread convection to the west from the previous overnight and morning hours, brought significant cloud cover to the eastern mid-Atlantic for much of the day on the 29th. This was mainly in the form of mid and high level cloud cover, however, and temperatures still warmed to near seasonal levels behind the warm front, allowing moderate to strong instability to build. Meanwhile, the robust surface and upper level disturbances approaching the region brought both strong forcing and unseasonably strong wind shear. By mid-afternoon, an unusually favorable convective environment existed over eastern Pennsylvania and New Jersey.||The result of this environment was a rare tornado outbreak across the area. Initially, during the mid-afternoon hours, mixed convective modes over east-central Pennsylvania produced a couple instances of damaging wind and brief tornadoes as storms attempted to overcome cloud cover and some light stratiform rain in the area. With time, as storms moved into better instability and a less contaminated environment over far eastern Pennsylvania and New Jersey, they took on more supercellular structures. Multiple cyclic supercells would go on to produce several tornadoes, three of them strong (EF2+) over eastern Pennsylvania and New Jersey. Convection persisted past the sunset hour with little to no loss in intensity due to the strong forcing. The only thing that put an end to the outbreak was the coastline, as storms moved offshore towards 9 to 10PM local time. Even offshore, one supercell continued to exhibit strong rotation and likely produced a strong waterspout for tens of miles over the Atlantic.||Fortunately, and miraculously, no serious injuries or loss of life occurred as a result of this outbreak. With the passage of a cold front late on the evening of the 29th, cooler, drier, and much less active weather would grace the region for several days as clean-up efforts began.

This front, combined with the remains of widespread convection to the west from the previous overnight and morning hours, brought significant cloud cover to the eastern mid-Atlantic for much of the day on the 29th. This was mainly in the form of mid and high level cloud cover, however, and temperatures still warmed to near seasonal levels behind the warm front, allowing moderate to strong instability to build. Meanwhile, the robust surface and upper level disturbances approaching the region brought both strong forcing and unseasonably strong wind shear. By mid-afternoon, an unusually favorable convective environment existed over eastern Pennsylvania and New Jersey.||The result of this environment was a rare tornado outbreak across the area. Initially, during the mid-afternoon hours, mixed convective modes over east-central Pennsylvania produced a couple instances of damaging wind and brief tornadoes as storms attempted to overcome cloud cover and some light stratiform rain in the area. With time, as storms moved into better instability and a less contaminated environment over far eastern Pennsylvania and New Jersey, they took on more supercellular structures. Multiple cyclic supercells would go on to produce several tornadoes, three of them strong (EF2+) over eastern Pennsylvania and New Jersey. Convection persisted past the sunset hour with little to no loss in intensity due to the strong forcing. The only thing that put an end to the outbreak was the coastline, as storms moved offshore towards 9 to 10PM local time. Even offshore, one supercell continued to exhibit strong rotation and likely produced a strong waterspout for tens of miles over the Atlantic.||Fortunately, and miraculously, no serious injuries or loss of life occurred as a result of this outbreak. With the passage of a cold front late on the evening of the 29th, cooler, drier, and much less active weather would grace the region for several days as clean-up efforts began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed along Neck Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa approached the mid-Atlantic states from the south on July 8. Downstream of Elsa, a warm and moist environment was present. A slow moving frontal boundary also resided over northern New Jersey, northern Pennsylvania, and into New York. This frontal system provided forcing for convective development over the mid-Atlantic, in fairly close proximity but unrelated to Tropical Storm Elsa. With strong instability and weak to moderate wind shear, some of the storms became severe as they organized into linear and multi-cellular clusters. Deep layer southerly flow in advance of Elsa caused storms to move northward as opposed to a more typical eastward motion. Damaging wind was the main hazard associated with these storms, with several strong downbursts noted. Storm activity eventually weakened and moved north of the region by evening as Elsa moved in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a power pole and started a fire on Mount Vernon Rd. Time estimated from radar.

A weak cold front dissipated west of the mid-Atlantic on June 4. However, a lingering surface trough slowly moved through the region. With a lingering warm and moist air mass in place, the surface trough caused convection to develop, starting early in the afternoon. Despite weak wind shear, storms steadily increased in organization, aided by favorable mesoscale interactions. Several storms would go on to pose a threat for damaging winds and hail. Storms eventually coalesced into multiple linear segments which posed a continued wind damage threat. Storms moved offshore by early to mid evening, with a drier air mass behind them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of Tropical Storm Elsa, a warm and humid air mass remained in place over the mid-Atlantic. A cold front approached the region late in the day, triggering a round of showers and thunderstorms. The previous night's rain and some synoptic subsidence behind Elsa limited instability somewhat, and shear was only weak to moderate. This kept much of the convection below severe limits. However, a couple of storms did become severe and produced damaging winds. Storm activity weakened and moved offshore in the later evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires in Franklin Twp. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough and associated cold front moved through the mid-Atlantic on November 13. This was a very compact but unusually potent disturbance which was preceded by a broken line of showers and thunderstorms. Strong wind fields and lift helped to generate convection, with a considerable amount of lightning observed especially given the time of year. Storms peaked in intensity further northeast, near Long Island and southern New England where a significant severe weather event occurred. Over New Jersey, the main impact was hail, which was mostly sub-severe but locally reached severe criteria. A period of strong non-thunderstorm winds then overspread the region as the cold front moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported as mostly dime size but up to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough and associated cold front moved through the mid-Atlantic on November 13. This was a very compact but unusually potent disturbance which was preceded by a broken line of showers and thunderstorms. Strong wind fields and lift helped to generate convection, with a considerable amount of lightning observed especially given the time of year. Storms peaked in intensity further northeast, near Long Island and southern New England where a significant severe weather event occurred. Over New Jersey, the main impact was hail, which was mostly sub-severe but locally reached severe criteria. A period of strong non-thunderstorm winds then overspread the region as the cold front moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to half dollar size was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near record warm air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on June 30. Combined with fairly high humidity, moderate to strong instability built over the region. An approaching surface trough acted to trigger scattered thunderstorms over the area during the afternoon and evening hours. Shear was not very strong, which limited overall storm organization. However, some storms did become strong to severe and produced instances of damaging winds. Convective activity diminished during the later evening hours with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on NJ-34 near Newman Springs Rd. Several power outages were reported in the area as well. Some damage was likely due to outflow winds. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Ida impacted the mid-Atlantic on September 1, 2021. As it moved northward, Ida began transitioning to a strong mid-latitude cyclone with pronounced frontal features, but with continued tropical moisture. On a regional level, the remnants of Ida became one of the most severe natural disasters in US history, due to a combination of several tornadoes, some of them strong, as well as catastrophic flooding over a large and densely populated area. The severe weather threat unfolded as the system's developing warm front lifted northward from the Delmarva region into Pennsylvania and New Jersey. South of the warm front, partial clearing and moderate to strong instability developed. Meanwhile, very strong shear and forcing, along with tropical moisture, were present within the warm sector. The result was widespread convection in the form of both supercell and quasi-linear thunderstorms. A number of storms became severe across the region, producing several tornadoes and other instances of wind damage. This continued an exceptionally active stretch of high end severe weather in the region, coming barely a month after the tornado outbreak of July 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After cycling from an earlier tornado near Mullica Hill, NJ, a |supercell thunderstorm produced an additional tornado in |Burlington Township, NJ near the township line with Edgewater |Park, NJ. Some evidence of RFD winds was prevalent southward |through the Capitol Hill neighborhood of Edgewater Park with one |pine tree being uprooted and several small branches being brought |down all the way south to Pennington Park in Delanco Township, |NJ,but damage was too sparse to be noted as tornadic. As the |tornado touched down, numerous hard and softwood trees were |uprooted along Jennifer Lane in a neighborhood just off of Beverly|Road in Burlington Township. A large branch was also brought down|on a vehicle at the intersection of Jennifer Lane and Squire Way.|Nearby, a pine tree was brought down, breaking a window to a |residence further west on Jennifer Lane with a small Sycamore tree|also being snapped at the base a few houses down the road. ||As the tornado crossed Shive Place and Handler Drive, several |trees were snapped and large branches brought down. Soffit damage |was also observed to a couple of homes nearby. Perhaps the most |impressive damage was observed along Beverly Road near the |intersection with Handler Drive where resident noted that nearly |thirty trees were either uprooted or snapped. Observations seemed |to agree with that statement with crews working to repair downed |power lines as well.||The tornado then proceeded towards the Delaware River as it took |a slight turn towards the north. With only a steel and power plant|lying beneath it-these two structurally sound facilities did not |appear to sustain any visible damage despite a rather impressive |viral Tweet with a video of the tornado passing across the |Burlington-Bristol Bridge. Driving across the bridge for this very|survey, no damage was noted given the structural integrity of the|feature. The tornado continued into Bucks County, Pennsylvania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rainfall associated with the remnants of Hurricane Ida overspread northeast New Jersey during the evening of September 1 and continued through the early morning hours of September 2. Rainfall totals ranged from 5-8+ inches across much of the region, with much of that rain falling in just a few hours. The ASOS at Newark Airport recorded 8.44 inches of rain, while the ASOS at Teterboro Airport recorded 7.14 inches. A COOP observer in Harrison recorded 8.72 inches of rain. This resulted in widespread flash flooding leading to numerous road closures and water rescues in addition to extensive river flooding. Eight people died as a result of the flash flooding and one person remains missing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hackensack River at River Vale exceeded its minor flood stage of 5.5 feet at 11:30pm EDT. The river then continued to rise quickly, surpassing moderate flood stage (6.5 feet) by 12:15am EDT September 2nd and major flood stage (8.0 feet) by 12:30am EDT. The river then crested at a height of 11.05 feet at 4:00am EDT September 2nd. After falling through much of the morning, the river experienced a secondary crest of 10.73 feet at 3:45pm EDT September 2nd. The river did not fall back below flood stage until 11:45am EDT on September 3rd.||The crest of 11.05 feet is the third highest on record at this location behind crests of 12.26 feet in August 2011 and 12.19 feet in April 2007.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dissipated west of the mid-Atlantic on June 4. However, a lingering surface trough slowly moved through the region. With a lingering warm and moist air mass in place, the surface trough caused convection to develop, starting early in the afternoon. Despite weak wind shear, storms steadily increased in organization, aided by favorable mesoscale interactions. Several storms would go on to pose a threat for damaging winds and hail. Storms eventually coalesced into multiple linear segments which posed a continued wind damage threat. Storms moved offshore by early to mid evening, with a drier air mass behind them.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed near Sunrise Trail. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid air mass remained in place over the mid-Atlantic on June 9, following several days of unusually hot and humid conditions for the season. Similar to the previous day, afternoon thunderstorm development occurred in the mid-Atlantic mainly due to the influence of a weak cold front to the north and a weak surface trough. Like the previous day, storms were disorganized due to weak wind fields and a lack of forcing. However, the strong to extreme instability allowed some storms to briefly become severe with a threat for damaging winds, hail, and torrential rain. Convection gradually dissipated during the evening hours with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on an apartment building off Sullivan Way. Time estimated from radar.

The crest of 10.14 feet is the highest on record for this location, exceeding the previous record crest of 9.97 feet in September 1999 due to Hurricane Floyd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rainfall associated with the remnants of Hurricane Ida overspread northeast New Jersey during the evening of September 1 and continued through the early morning hours of September 2. Rainfall totals ranged from 5-8+ inches across much of the region, with much of that rain falling in just a few hours. The ASOS at Newark Airport recorded 8.44 inches of rain, while the ASOS at Teterboro Airport recorded 7.14 inches. A COOP observer in Harrison recorded 8.72 inches of rain. This resulted in widespread flash flooding leading to numerous road closures and water rescues in addition to extensive river flooding. Eight people died as a result of the flash flooding and one person remains missing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rescues were ongoing for several occupied stranded cars on Route 17 near Racetrack Road in Ho-Ho-Kus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dissipated west of the mid-Atlantic on June 4. However, a lingering surface trough slowly moved through the region. With a lingering warm and moist air mass in place, the surface trough caused convection to develop, starting early in the afternoon. Despite weak wind shear, storms steadily increased in organization, aided by favorable mesoscale interactions. Several storms would go on to pose a threat for damaging winds and hail. Storms eventually coalesced into multiple linear segments which posed a continued wind damage threat. Storms moved offshore by early to mid evening, with a drier air mass behind them.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed near West Shore Dr. Time estimated from radar.

It then continued to rise, climbing above moderate flood stage (9.0 feet) at 11:15pm before cresting at a height of 10.46 feet at 3:00am EDT on September 2nd. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near record warm air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on June 30. Combined with fairly high humidity, moderate to strong instability built over the region. An approaching surface trough acted to trigger scattered thunderstorms over the area during the afternoon and evening hours. Shear was not very strong, which limited overall storm organization. However, some storms did become strong to severe and produced instances of damaging winds. Convective activity diminished during the later evening hours with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of downed trees and wires near Englishtown and north of Freehold. Some damage was likely caused by outflow winds. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rainfall associated with the remnants of Hurricane Ida overspread northeast New Jersey during the evening of September 1 and continued through the early morning hours of September 2. Rainfall totals ranged from 5-8+ inches across much of the region, with much of that rain falling in just a few hours. The ASOS at Newark Airport recorded 8.44 inches of rain, while the ASOS at Teterboro Airport recorded 7.14 inches. A COOP observer in Harrison recorded 8.72 inches of rain. This resulted in widespread flash flooding leading to numerous road closures and water rescues in addition to extensive river flooding. Eight people died as a result of the flash flooding and one person remains missing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pascack River at Park Ridge rose above its minor flood stage of 5.2 feet at 9:15pm EDT. It then continued to rise, surpassing moderate flood stage (6.2 feet) at 9:45pm EDT and major flood stage (7.2 feet) at 11:30pm EDT. The river then crested at a height of 7.44 feet at 12:15am EDT September 2nd. The river then gradually receded overnight, falling back below minor flood stage at 4:30am EDT September 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rainfall associated with the remnants of Hurricane Ida overspread northeast New Jersey during the evening of September 1 and continued through the early morning hours of September 2. Rainfall totals ranged from 5-8+ inches across much of the region, with much of that rain falling in just a few hours. The ASOS at Newark Airport recorded 8.44 inches of rain, while the ASOS at Teterboro Airport recorded 7.14 inches. A COOP observer in Harrison recorded 8.72 inches of rain. This resulted in widespread flash flooding leading to numerous road closures and water rescues in addition to extensive river flooding. Eight people died as a result of the flash flooding and one person remains missing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rahway River near Springfield rose above its minor flood stage of 7.0 feet at 8:30pm EDT. It then continued to rise, surpassing moderate flood stage (8.5 feet) at 9:15pm EDT and major flood stage (10.5 feet) at 11:45pm EDT before cresting at a height of 10.81 feet at 1:15am EDT on September 2nd. The crest of 10.81 feet is the second highest on record, behind only a crest of 10.88 feet in August 2011 associated with Tropical Storm Irene. The river then gradually fell through the overnight hours, eventually fallign back below minor flood stage at 12:45pm EDT on September 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a more than two week stretch of dry weather, a cold front approached the mid-Atlantic on May 26. Ahead of the front, an unseasonably hot and humid air mass developed, with high temperatures near to above 90 degrees in much of the region. This contributed to strong instability, while wind shear was more moderate. Multiple rounds of convection affected the region, triggered by a pre-frontal trough and a couple of upper level impulses ahead of the front. A fairly widespread severe weather event resulted, with damaging winds by far the most widespread hazard. Numerous instances of downed trees and power lines and some property damage were reported. Thunderstorms during the evening hours were also prolific lightning producers. The final round of storms finally moved offshore close to midnight after several hours of severe weather in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and wires were downed near Deerfield Ln. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain associated with Post Tropical Cyclone Ida produced moderate to major flooding along the tidal Delaware River on the night of September 1.  There was another round of moderate flooding on the night of September 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries in Camden County, inundating adjacent roadways and properties.  The tide gauge at Bridesburg (Philadelphia) reached a level of 10.16 feet Mean Lower Low Water.  The tide gauge at the Coast Guard Station in Philadelphia reached a level of 9.69 feet Mean Lower Low Water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Ohio Valley and Middle Atlantic on Sunday, January 31, 2021. The low then consolidated along the Middle Atlantic coast early on Monday, February 1, 2021 and intensified as it slowly tracked to the north-northeast. The low meandered slowly north as it moved along the northeast and New England coast later on Monday February 1, 2021 through Wednesday, February 3, 2021. The low brought a major winter storm to northeast New Jersey, with the most significant impacts occurring on Monday, February 1, 2021. 3 to 6 inches of snow had already accumulated by the start of the morning commute. Near blizzard conditions then occurred through the rest of the morning into the early afternoon with snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour. Strong winds combined with these heavy snow rates to produce visibilities one quarter mile or less at times. The heavier bands of snow lifted north across the rest of northeast New Jersey in the afternoon and evening. Lighter snow bands then lingered intermittently through the early morning hours on February 3, 2021. Snowfall totals ranged 15 to 20 inches of snow with some locations receiving around two feet. Winds gusted 40 to 55 mph at times on Monday, February 1, 2021, which caused power outages. Travel was also severely impacted as many flights were cancelled and travel by train was suspended. COVID testing and vaccination sites were closed. Impacts were limited on February 2, 2021 and February 3, 2021 as the snow was largely light.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 19.0 inches of snow in Berkeley Heights, 17.5 inches of snow in Garwood, and 17.2 inches of snow in Westfield. A 2 inch per hour snowfall rate was measured at the nearby Newark Liberty International Airport between 10 am and 11 am and between 11 am and 12 pm.  The nearby Newark Liberty International Airport ASOS measured a 41 mph wind gust at 2:51 pm on February 1, 2021.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in the tidal communities of Middlesex County on the night of May 28-29.  The tide gauge at Perth Amboy reached 8.48 feet MLLW.

The heavier bands of snow lifted north across the rest of northeast New Jersey in the afternoon and evening. Lighter snow bands then lingered intermittently through the early morning hours on February 3, 2021. Snowfall totals ranged 15 to 20 inches of snow with some locations receiving around two feet. Winds gusted 40 to 55 mph at times on Monday, February 1, 2021, which caused power outages. Travel was also severely impacted as many flights were cancelled and travel by train was suspended. COVID testing and vaccination sites were closed. Impacts were limited on February 2, 2021 and February 3, 2021 as the snow was largely light.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Bloomingdale reported 26.2 inches of snow. The public reported 24.7 inches of snow 3 miles northeast of West Milford. A trained spotter 3 miles west-southwest of West Milford reported 24.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure resulted in a prolonged period of onshore flow along the coast of New Jersey from May 28th through the 30th.  Moderate coastal flooding occurred along the northern part of the New Jersey coast on the night of May 28-29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration wintry weather event occurred on Thursday, February 18, 2021 through Friday, February 19, 2021. A frontal wave of low pressure developed off the Middle Atlantic coast on February 18, 2021. The frontal wave combined with a strong upper level to produce a brief period of moderate to heavy snowfall during the morning and early afternoon on Thursday, February 18, 2021. The snow also mixed with sleet at times during the late morning and early afternoon hours across portions of northeast New Jersey. Snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour occurred during this time frame. The precipitation became light and changed to spotty freezing rain and freezing drizzle during the late afternoon through the overnight hours on February 18, 2021. The overall weather pattern did not change to much on Friday, February 19, 2021. Weak waves of low pressure continued to track along the frontal zone off the Middle Atlantic coast, combining with the lingering strong jet stream aloft to produce periods of mainly light snow. The light snow persisted into the early evening hours before tapering off during the evening on Friday, February 19, 2021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Wayne reported 5.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on June 8. A weak upper level disturbance helped to initiate thunderstorms over the area during the afternoon hours. Storms were lacking in organization due to weak wind fields and limited forcing, and mainly took the form of single cells and disorganized multi-cells. However, due to the strong to extreme instability present, some of the storms did become strong to severe and produced downbursts containing damaging winds and some hail. Storms also produced torrential rain and areas of flash flooding. Convection waned during the evening hours with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with weakening Hurricane Henri caused significant flash flooding in New Jersey on the morning of August 22.  Most of the flood damage was confined to the central part of the state.  Rainfall amounts were as high as 4.0 to 9.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Musconetcong River overflowed its banks, flooding a section of NJ Route 173 in Greenwich Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day excessive heat event occurred across much of the mid-Atlantic near the end of June 2021. High temperatures in the mid to locally upper 90s combined with dew points in the upper 60s to near 70 caused heat index values to reach 105 to 110 over much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached 105F on both June 29 and 30, especially across the eastern half of the county, and locally reached 110F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing shortwave combined with a surface trough resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms across northeast New Jersey during the early afternoon hours. These thunderstorms developed in an environment with weak steering flow and high precipitable water values, resulting in several reports of flash flooding. Based on upper air soundings from Upton, NY, precipitable water values increased from around 1.75 inches on the morning of June 8th to just over 2 inches that evening. Rainfall amounts varied widely across the area. The ASOS at the Teterboro Airport reported 1.66 inches of rain, while the Newark Airport ASOS reported 2.19 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An occupied vehicle was stranded in flood waters at the intersection of Pleasantview Avenue and Summit Avenue in Hackensack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed over eastern New Jersey on August 30. Marginal environmental conditions precluded any severe weather in association with these storms. However, they approached several locations along the New Jersey Shore during the mid to late afternoon. Beaches were crowded on what had previously been a sunny and warm day, creating a dangerous situation due to the lightning being produced by the approaching storms. Sadly, a Seaside Park lifeguard was killed and several others in the same area were injured by a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven individuals were struck by lightning in South Seaside Park, with one fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward moving cold front stalled across northern New Jersey and northeast Pennsylvania early on September 13. Warm and humid conditions were present south of the front. An upper level disturbance moving through the westerly flow aloft helped to increase wind shear. Combined with the instability, this disturbance caused a line of thunderstorms to move through the region during the evening hours of September 13. This line of storms became severe, with a number of reports of damaging wind along it. The line moved offshore and out of the region later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at Keansburg Weatherflow station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As strengthening low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes on March 28, a strong cold front approached the mid-Atlantic coast. A narrow ribbon of modest instability developed ahead of the front. With strong wind fields in place and strong forcing from the cold front, a line of convection developed. The minimal instability caused this to be a low-topped convective line with little, though not zero, lightning. However, the strong forcing and wind fields caused the line to become a fairly prolific producer of straight line wind damage. A number of severe wind gusts were measured, with numerous reports of tree, utility, and some structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some large trees were downed in Matawan. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High fire danger conditions were in place across much of the mid-Atlantic on March 14, 2021. A series of cold fronts were in the process of moving through the region, all of which were moving through without producing any precipitation. On March 14, in between a couple of these fronts, relative humidity values dropped below 30%, and winds were gusting to near 40 mph. This combined with dry antecedent conditions prompted the issuance of Red Flag Warnings. Several brush fires were reported, and a damaging wildfire occurred in eastern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire, determined to be intentionally set, broke out in Lakewood, NJ on the early afternoon of March 14. The fire began in an area of woods, but quickly spread to a nearby industrial park, damaging or destroying several buildings. For the next several hours, strong westerly winds pushed the fast spreading fire eastward, mainly over wooded areas but in close proximity to densely populated areas of Lakewood and Brick. Unusually extreme fire behavior for the region, including multiple instances of downwind spotting, were observed. Numerous state and local fire companies assisted in bringing the fire under control. Only slow containment progress was made for most of the day on the 14th, with better progress by evening as weather conditions became more favorable for firefighters. Remaining hotspots had been extinguished within about 24 hours of the initial start. Approximately 170 acres were burned. One firefighter was seriously injured after suffering a cardiac event as crews fought the wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred over the region as high pressure built in from the west behind a strengthening coastal low pressure system heading northeast after passing well to the south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weakening low pressure tracking out of the Mississippi Valley passed west of the mid-Atlantic on January 26. Secondary low pressure developed near the Delmarva region late on the 26th, tracking east-northeastward and passing south of New England. Upper level ridging prevented much intensification of this secondary low, which combined with the decaying primary low to bring a poorly organized weather system to the mid-Atlantic. A light wintry mix of precipitation fell, including a mix of snow and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of light snow and freezing rain fell. The Sussex Airport (KFWN) ASOS measured 0.01 inches of ice accretion, with 0.5 to 1.5 inches of snow observed throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the mid-Atlantic from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Low pressure tracking out of the Ohio Valley weakened as it moved slowly eastward on the 31st. Meanwhile, as that low weakened, secondary low pressure began to develop off the coast of the Carolinas and tracked northward, just off the mid-Atlantic coast. The low steadily intensified as it moved up the coast in a classic ���Miller B��� storm evolution. The result was a significant and unusually long duration winter storm. Overrunning precipitation associated with the initial low over the Ohio Valley impacted the region on the 31st. A cold antecedent air mass allowed most of this precipitation to fall as snow. This produced widespread snow accumulation, though the overrunning precipitation was mostly of the light to moderate variety. Some areas experienced a lull in precipitation on the overnight of the 31st as the secondary low began to develop and a dry slot overspread a large area. In addition, a surge of warmer air both at the surface and aloft caused precipitation to change to sleet or rain in some areas, especially locations farthest south and east such as the Delmarva peninsula and southern New Jersey. However, as the secondary low strengthened, heavy precipitation developed on February 1, with intense banding and areas of extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour in northern New Jersey and portions of eastern Pennsylvania. Precipitation eventually turned back to snow all the way to the coastline as colder air was drawn in from the north. The secondary low pressure nearly stalled off the mid-Atlantic coast, causing snow to continue into February 2, although by then snowfall rates were much lighter and additional accumulation was limited. Areas where precipitation remained all snow and that experienced the heavy banding saw extreme snowfall totals, with isolated amounts of near 3 feet of snow and a fairly widespread swath of in excess of 2 feet. Snow flurries even continued into February 3 around the backside of the slow moving low���s large circulation, before it finally departed and brought an end to one of the region���s most notable snow events in years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Hopewell Twp reported 4.0 inches on snow from the snow on January 31. Additional mixed precipitation occurred on February 1 and 2 but produced little additional snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weakening low pressure tracking out of the Mississippi Valley passed west of the mid-Atlantic on January 26. Secondary low pressure developed near the Delmarva region late on the 26th, tracking east-northeastward and passing south of New England. Upper level ridging prevented much intensification of this secondary low, which combined with the decaying primary low to bring a poorly organized weather system to the mid-Atlantic. A light wintry mix of precipitation fell, including a mix of snow and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trenton Mercer Airport (KTTN) ASOS reported several hours of rain at 32 degrees, which combined with observations from surrounding locations suggest areas of light ice accretion likely occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of upper level disturbances passing over the mid-Atlantic brought a somewhat long duration but fairly low impact winter weather event to the area from the morning of January 3 into the predawn hours of January 4. Multiple batches of warm-advection driven precipitation affected the region in association with these disturbances as weak surface cyclogenesis began to occur just offshore. Surface temperatures were marginally cold enough to support snow or freezing rain across interior areas. A lack of deep cold air and the disorganized nature of most of the precipitation generally limited accumulations and impacts, but some areas saw a few inches of snow and some light glazes of freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow and freezing rain fell. A trained spotter in Vernon reported 3.5 inches of snow.

Multiple batches of warm-advection driven precipitation affected the region in association with these disturbances as weak surface cyclogenesis began to occur just offshore. Surface temperatures were marginally cold enough to support snow or freezing rain across interior areas. A lack of deep cold air and the disorganized nature of most of the precipitation generally limited accumulations and impacts, but some areas saw a few inches of snow and some light glazes of freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow and freezing rain fell. A CoCoRaHS report of 1.7 inches of snow was received from near Frelinghuysen Twp, and surrounding observations suggest areas of a light glaze of ice also likely occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking through the Great Lakes began to redevelop over the interior mid-Atlantic on January 1. Meanwhile, surface high pressure retreated northward from New England into southern Canada. This created a typical cold air damming regime over the mid-Atlantic as warm air advected in aloft while cold air remained trapped at the surface. This caused precipitation over interior portions of the mid-Atlantic to begin as sleet and freezing rain before eventually changing over to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet and freezing rain fell. The Sussex Airport ASOS (KFWN) measured 0.10 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the NC/VA coast during the morning on Sunday, February 7, 2021. The low then tracked northeast away from the Middle Atlantic through the afternoon as it intensified, then well south and east of Long Island in the late afternoon and evening. A band of moderate to heavy snow developed across the urban corridor in northeast New Jersey during the morning and early afternoon hours. Snowfall rates within this band were around one to as high as two inches per hour at times. Surrounding this moderate to heavy band was a steady snowfall through much of the day. Snowfall accumulation ranged from 4 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer in Harrison reported 5.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance accompanied by a weak wave of surface low pressure passed south of the Delmarva peninsula in the early hours of February 11. Precipitation blossomed ahead of and north of this disturbance. Over the mid-Atlantic, temperatures were cold enough for this to fall as a fluffy light snow in most areas. The relatively weak nature of the system combined with considerable dry air in place limited the amount of precipitation that fell. However, good snow to liquid ratios and the predawn timing of most of the snow allowed for efficient accumulation, with a widespread area of 3 to 5 inches of snow over the mid-Atlantic and some amounts locally a little higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell, with a small local maximum in Hunterdon County. A trained spotter in Ringoes reported 3.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved slowly northeastward over the waters off Delaware and New Jersey on February 1-2, 2021.  The brisk onshore flow resulted in two consecutive high tide cycles with widespread moderate flooding along the New Jersey coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate coastal flooding occurred in the tidal communities of Cape May County during the morning and early afternoon of February 1.  There were numerous road closures.  The water level reached 6.67 feet MLLW at Ocean City, 7.35 feet MLLW at Sea Isle City, 7.33 feet MLLW at Avalon, and 7.42 feet MLLW at Stone Harbor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the mid-Atlantic from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Low pressure tracking out of the Ohio Valley weakened as it moved slowly eastward on the 31st. Meanwhile, as that low weakened, secondary low pressure began to develop off the coast of the Carolinas and tracked northward, just off the mid-Atlantic coast. The low steadily intensified as it moved up the coast in a classic ���Miller B��� storm evolution. The result was a significant and unusually long duration winter storm. Overrunning precipitation associated with the initial low over the Ohio Valley impacted the region on the 31st. A cold antecedent air mass allowed most of this precipitation to fall as snow. This produced widespread snow accumulation, though the overrunning precipitation was mostly of the light to moderate variety. Some areas experienced a lull in precipitation on the overnight of the 31st as the secondary low began to develop and a dry slot overspread a large area. In addition, a surge of warmer air both at the surface and aloft caused precipitation to change to sleet or rain in some areas, especially locations farthest south and east such as the Delmarva peninsula and southern New Jersey. However, as the secondary low strengthened, heavy precipitation developed on February 1, with intense banding and areas of extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour in northern New Jersey and portions of eastern Pennsylvania. Precipitation eventually turned back to snow all the way to the coastline as colder air was drawn in from the north. The secondary low pressure nearly stalled off the mid-Atlantic coast, causing snow to continue into February 2, although by then snowfall rates were much lighter and additional accumulation was limited. Areas where precipitation remained all snow and that experienced the heavy banding saw extreme snowfall totals, with isolated amounts of near 3 feet of snow and a fairly widespread swath of in excess of 2 feet. Snow flurries even continued into February 3 around the backside of the slow moving low���s large circulation, before it finally departed and brought an end to one of the region���s most notable snow events in years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Point Pleasant Beach reported a 63 mph wind gust. Numerous measured wind gusts over 50 mph were recorded along coastal Ocean County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the mid-Atlantic on February 22, with a developing wave of low pressure tracking northeast along the front just inland from the Atlantic coast. This fast moving system brought a period of steady precipitation to the region mainly during the morning and midday hours on the 22nd. A cold air mass was in place ahead of the system, causing precipitation to start as snow. Southerly winds ahead of the cold front brought warmer air into the region, causing a change from snow to rain in many areas. Despite it being a fairly atypical setup for wintry precipitation in the region, a brief but intense period of snow affected much of eastern Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey. Several inches of heavy, wet snow fell in just a few hours, causing considerable disruption on area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell, then changed to rain. A trained spotter near the Trenton Mercer Airport reported 2.5 inches of snow, and the airport ASOS (KTTN) also reported a trace of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance accompanied by a weak wave of surface low pressure passed south of the Delmarva peninsula in the early hours of February 11. Precipitation blossomed ahead of and north of this disturbance. Over the mid-Atlantic, temperatures were cold enough for this to fall as a fluffy light snow in most areas. The relatively weak nature of the system combined with considerable dry air in place limited the amount of precipitation that fell. However, good snow to liquid ratios and the predawn timing of most of the snow allowed for efficient accumulation, with a widespread area of 3 to 5 inches of snow over the mid-Atlantic and some amounts locally a little higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell. A report of 3.8 inches of snow was received from Brigantine.

The Atlantic City International Airport reported 5.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the mid-Atlantic from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Low pressure tracking out of the Ohio Valley weakened as it moved slowly eastward on the 31st. Meanwhile, as that low weakened, secondary low pressure began to develop off the coast of the Carolinas and tracked northward, just off the mid-Atlantic coast. The low steadily intensified as it moved up the coast in a classic ���Miller B��� storm evolution. The result was a significant and unusually long duration winter storm. Overrunning precipitation associated with the initial low over the Ohio Valley impacted the region on the 31st. A cold antecedent air mass allowed most of this precipitation to fall as snow. This produced widespread snow accumulation, though the overrunning precipitation was mostly of the light to moderate variety. Some areas experienced a lull in precipitation on the overnight of the 31st as the secondary low began to develop and a dry slot overspread a large area. In addition, a surge of warmer air both at the surface and aloft caused precipitation to change to sleet or rain in some areas, especially locations farthest south and east such as the Delmarva peninsula and southern New Jersey. However, as the secondary low strengthened, heavy precipitation developed on February 1, with intense banding and areas of extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour in northern New Jersey and portions of eastern Pennsylvania. Precipitation eventually turned back to snow all the way to the coastline as colder air was drawn in from the north. The secondary low pressure nearly stalled off the mid-Atlantic coast, causing snow to continue into February 2, although by then snowfall rates were much lighter and additional accumulation was limited. Areas where precipitation remained all snow and that experienced the heavy banding saw extreme snowfall totals, with isolated amounts of near 3 feet of snow and a fairly widespread swath of in excess of 2 feet. Snow flurries even continued into February 3 around the backside of the slow moving low���s large circulation, before it finally departed and brought an end to one of the region���s most notable snow events in years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer in Point Pleasant Beach reported a storm total of 4.1 inches of snow. Most of this fell during mixed precipitation changing to snow on February 1, but an inch or two had likely occurred during light snow on January 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure tracked offshore of the mid-Atlantic on February 13, spreading some light precipitation into the region. A surge of warm air aloft prevented the light precipitation from falling as snow. However, strong high pressure to the north promoted a cold air damming environment with sub-freezing air trapped at the surface. This led to most of the precipitation falling as freezing rain, with some generally light ice accretion across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace of freezing rain was reported at the Trenton Mercer Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving wave of low pressure tracked northeastward off the Atlantic coast on February 7. The low steadily intensified as it gained latitude, and a sizeable shield of banded precipitation developed in its northwest quadrant. This resulted in a fairly short duration period of precipitation over the eastern mid-Atlantic, but with precipitation rates that were moderate to heavy at times. The storm produced a wide swath of several inches of snow, with considerable local variations in the snow totals. The variations were due to both distinct banding features as well as marginally cold temperatures especially near the coast, which caused precipitation to start as rain in some areas. The storm departed the region by the late afternoon of February 7 after producing a general 4 to 8 inches of snow, with some localized totals that were a little higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell in the county, with heavier snow remaining to the south. A trained spotter in Hopatcong reported 3.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure passed offshore of the mid-Atlantic on February 18. Another weak, secondary wave of low pressure tracked along a similar path along a frontal zone trailing behind the primary low on the 19th. Strong high pressure over New England provided a cold air mass over the mid-Atlantic, leading to wintry precipitation as these areas of low pressure tracked offshore. The heaviest precipitation occurred with the primary wave on the 18th, with many areas seeing snow, some locally heavy, with a change to sleet and rain towards coastal areas. A narrow but intense band of precipitation provided a maximum in snowfall over portions of southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. By the overnight of the 18th and into the 19th, precipitation became lighter, with a mix of light snow and light freezing rain or drizzle providing some additional accumulations and impacts. The system finally pulled away entirely by the late afternoon and evening of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell. The highest report, which came over a period of around 36 hours with multiple starts and stops, was 5.6 inches in Andover. Other reports of 3 to 4 inches were received from the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration wintry weather event occurred on Thursday, February 18, 2021 through Friday, February 19, 2021. A frontal wave of low pressure developed off the Middle Atlantic coast on February 18, 2021. The frontal wave combined with a strong upper level to produce a brief period of moderate to heavy snowfall during the morning and early afternoon on Thursday, February 18, 2021. The snow also mixed with sleet at times during the late morning and early afternoon hours across portions of northeast New Jersey. Snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour occurred during this time frame. The precipitation became light and changed to spotty freezing rain and freezing drizzle during the late afternoon through the overnight hours on February 18, 2021. The overall weather pattern did not change to much on Friday, February 19, 2021. Weak waves of low pressure continued to track along the frontal zone off the Middle Atlantic coast, combining with the lingering strong jet stream aloft to produce periods of mainly light snow. The light snow persisted into the early evening hours before tapering off during the evening on Friday, February 19, 2021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Bloomingdale reported 4.8 inches of snow and a trace of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure tracked offshore of the mid-Atlantic on February 13, spreading some light precipitation into the region. A surge of warm air aloft prevented the light precipitation from falling as snow. However, strong high pressure to the north promoted a cold air damming environment with sub-freezing air trapped at the surface. This led to most of the precipitation falling as freezing rain, with some generally light ice accretion across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell, with Atlantic County seeing the highest amounts in the region. A COOP observer in Estell Manor reported 0.30 inches of ice accretion, and the Atlantic City International Airport ASOS reported 0.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving wave of low pressure tracked northeastward off the Atlantic coast on February 7. The low steadily intensified as it gained latitude, and a sizeable shield of banded precipitation developed in its northwest quadrant. This resulted in a fairly short duration period of precipitation over the eastern mid-Atlantic, but with precipitation rates that were moderate to heavy at times. The storm produced a wide swath of several inches of snow, with considerable local variations in the snow totals. The variations were due to both distinct banding features as well as marginally cold temperatures especially near the coast, which caused precipitation to start as rain in some areas. The storm departed the region by the late afternoon of February 7 after producing a general 4 to 8 inches of snow, with some localized totals that were a little higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light rain changed to snow across the county and became heavy at times. A trained spotter in Holmdel reported 7.0 inches of snow, with several additional reports of 5 to 7 inches in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure passed offshore of the mid-Atlantic on February 18. Another weak, secondary wave of low pressure tracked along a similar path along a frontal zone trailing behind the primary low on the 19th. Strong high pressure over New England provided a cold air mass over the mid-Atlantic, leading to wintry precipitation as these areas of low pressure tracked offshore. The heaviest precipitation occurred with the primary wave on the 18th, with many areas seeing snow, some locally heavy, with a change to sleet and rain towards coastal areas. A narrow but intense band of precipitation provided a maximum in snowfall over portions of southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. By the overnight of the 18th and into the 19th, precipitation became lighter, with a mix of light snow and light freezing rain or drizzle providing some additional accumulations and impacts. The system finally pulled away entirely by the late afternoon and evening of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wintry mixed precipitation fell, including a band of heavy snow which impacted the county on the morning of the 18th. A trained spotter in Manalapan Twp reported a storm total snowfall of 9.5 inches. A COOP observer near Milltown also reported 0.04 inches of ice accretion, mainly from light freezing rain and drizzle on the overnight of the 18th.

A trained spotter in Holmdel also reported 0.15 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration wintry weather event occurred on Thursday, February 18, 2021 through Friday, February 19, 2021. A frontal wave of low pressure developed off the Middle Atlantic coast on February 18, 2021. The frontal wave combined with a strong upper level to produce a brief period of moderate to heavy snowfall during the morning and early afternoon on Thursday, February 18, 2021. The snow also mixed with sleet at times during the late morning and early afternoon hours across portions of northeast New Jersey. Snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour occurred during this time frame. The precipitation became light and changed to spotty freezing rain and freezing drizzle during the late afternoon through the overnight hours on February 18, 2021. The overall weather pattern did not change to much on Friday, February 19, 2021. Weak waves of low pressure continued to track along the frontal zone off the Middle Atlantic coast, combining with the lingering strong jet stream aloft to produce periods of mainly light snow. The light snow persisted into the early evening hours before tapering off during the evening on Friday, February 19, 2021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and the public reported 4 to 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked just east of the Great Lakes from the overnight hours of February 15 into the morning of the 16th. The low produced widespread precipitation over the mid-Atlantic. The inland track of the low allowed warmer air to surge northward for locations east of the center. However, lingering sub-freezing surface temperatures were present in some areas following recent cold weather. This caused precipitation to fall as freezing rain in portions of the interior mid-Atlantic. A lack of high pressure to the north to lock in colder air caused a quick change to plain rain in many areas. However, portions of the interior, especially in the vicinity of higher terrain, experienced more significant icing as freezing rain lasted for longer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell. A trained spotter near Wantage Twp reported 0.38 inches of ice accretion, and the Sussex Airport (KFWN) ASOS reported 0.34 inches of accretion.

Lighter snow and sleet fell elsewhere, followed by light freezing rain and drizzle on the overnight of the 18th and a bit of additional light snow on the 19th. A storm total snowfall report of 6.4 inches was received from Delanco, and a trained spotter in Bordentown reported 6.3 inches. The South Jersey Regional Airport (KVAY) ASOS measured 0.15 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration wintry weather event occurred on Thursday, February 18, 2021 through Friday, February 19, 2021. A frontal wave of low pressure developed off the Middle Atlantic coast on February 18, 2021. The frontal wave combined with a strong upper level to produce a brief period of moderate to heavy snowfall during the morning and early afternoon on Thursday, February 18, 2021. The snow also mixed with sleet at times during the late morning and early afternoon hours across portions of northeast New Jersey. Snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour occurred during this time frame. The precipitation became light and changed to spotty freezing rain and freezing drizzle during the late afternoon through the overnight hours on February 18, 2021. The overall weather pattern did not change to much on Friday, February 19, 2021. Weak waves of low pressure continued to track along the frontal zone off the Middle Atlantic coast, combining with the lingering strong jet stream aloft to produce periods of mainly light snow. The light snow persisted into the early evening hours before tapering off during the evening on Friday, February 19, 2021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and the public reported 3 to 5 inches of snow. The Caldwell Airport ASOS recorded 0.14 inches of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the mid-Atlantic from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Low pressure tracking out of the Ohio Valley weakened as it moved slowly eastward on the 31st. Meanwhile, as that low weakened, secondary low pressure began to develop off the coast of the Carolinas and tracked northward, just off the mid-Atlantic coast. The low steadily intensified as it moved up the coast in a classic ���Miller B��� storm evolution. The result was a significant and unusually long duration winter storm. Overrunning precipitation associated with the initial low over the Ohio Valley impacted the region on the 31st. A cold antecedent air mass allowed most of this precipitation to fall as snow. This produced widespread snow accumulation, though the overrunning precipitation was mostly of the light to moderate variety. Some areas experienced a lull in precipitation on the overnight of the 31st as the secondary low began to develop and a dry slot overspread a large area. In addition, a surge of warmer air both at the surface and aloft caused precipitation to change to sleet or rain in some areas, especially locations farthest south and east such as the Delmarva peninsula and southern New Jersey. However, as the secondary low strengthened, heavy precipitation developed on February 1, with intense banding and areas of extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour in northern New Jersey and portions of eastern Pennsylvania. Precipitation eventually turned back to snow all the way to the coastline as colder air was drawn in from the north. The secondary low pressure nearly stalled off the mid-Atlantic coast, causing snow to continue into February 2, although by then snowfall rates were much lighter and additional accumulation was limited. Areas where precipitation remained all snow and that experienced the heavy banding saw extreme snowfall totals, with isolated amounts of near 3 feet of snow and a fairly widespread swath of in excess of 2 feet. Snow flurries even continued into February 3 around the backside of the slow moving low���s large circulation, before it finally departed and brought an end to one of the region���s most notable snow events in years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on January 31. Precipitation continued into February 1. It decreased in intensity and also mixed with rain and sleet for a time before changing back to snow later on February 1. Light snow continued into February 2. A storm total snowfall of 9.8 inches was reported in Pitman. Several other reports of 5 to 9 inches of snow were received from the long duration storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving wave of low pressure tracked northeastward off the Atlantic coast on February 7. The low steadily intensified as it gained latitude, and a sizeable shield of banded precipitation developed in its northwest quadrant. This resulted in a fairly short duration period of precipitation over the eastern mid-Atlantic, but with precipitation rates that were moderate to heavy at times. The storm produced a wide swath of several inches of snow, with considerable local variations in the snow totals. The variations were due to both distinct banding features as well as marginally cold temperatures especially near the coast, which caused precipitation to start as rain in some areas. The storm departed the region by the late afternoon of February 7 after producing a general 4 to 8 inches of snow, with some localized totals that were a little higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the area, though heavier banding avoided this region. Observations from surrounding areas suggest a general 2 to 3 inches of snow likely fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked just east of the Great Lakes from the overnight hours of February 15 into the morning of the 16th. The low produced widespread precipitation over the mid-Atlantic. The inland track of the low allowed warmer air to surge northward for locations east of the center. However, lingering sub-freezing surface temperatures were present in some areas following recent cold weather. This caused precipitation to fall as freezing rain in portions of the interior mid-Atlantic. A lack of high pressure to the north to lock in colder air caused a quick change to plain rain in many areas. However, portions of the interior, especially in the vicinity of higher terrain, experienced more significant icing as freezing rain lasted for longer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell. A report of 0.04 inches of ice accretion was received from Lake Hopatcong.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure passed offshore of the mid-Atlantic on February 18. Another weak, secondary wave of low pressure tracked along a similar path along a frontal zone trailing behind the primary low on the 19th. Strong high pressure over New England provided a cold air mass over the mid-Atlantic, leading to wintry precipitation as these areas of low pressure tracked offshore. The heaviest precipitation occurred with the primary wave on the 18th, with many areas seeing snow, some locally heavy, with a change to sleet and rain towards coastal areas. A narrow but intense band of precipitation provided a maximum in snowfall over portions of southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. By the overnight of the 18th and into the 19th, precipitation became lighter, with a mix of light snow and light freezing rain or drizzle providing some additional accumulations and impacts. The system finally pulled away entirely by the late afternoon and evening of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light wintry mixed precipitation fell over a prolonged period from the morning of the 18th through the evening of the 19th. No direct observations were received. Observations from surrounding areas suggest 2 to 3 inches of snow and sleet (mainly during the day on the 18th) and up to a tenth of an inch of glaze ice (mainly on the overnight of the 18th) likely occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave of low pressure tracked over the mid-Atlantic on February 9. The low was fast moving and had poor dynamic forcing associated with it, limiting precipitation production. However, the air mass was sufficiently cold for what precipitation did occur to fall as mainly snow over northern New Jersey and portions of eastern Pennsylvania, with some patchy light freezing rain and drizzle reported as well. Some light snow accumulations resulted mainly during the morning and midday hours of February 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light snow of 2 to 4 inches fell across the county. A report of 3.8 inches was received from Wantage Twp, with a nearby spotter reporting 3.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure passed offshore of the mid-Atlantic on February 18. Another weak, secondary wave of low pressure tracked along a similar path along a frontal zone trailing behind the primary low on the 19th. Strong high pressure over New England provided a cold air mass over the mid-Atlantic, leading to wintry precipitation as these areas of low pressure tracked offshore. The heaviest precipitation occurred with the primary wave on the 18th, with many areas seeing snow, some locally heavy, with a change to sleet and rain towards coastal areas. A narrow but intense band of precipitation provided a maximum in snowfall over portions of southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. By the overnight of the 18th and into the 19th, precipitation became lighter, with a mix of light snow and light freezing rain or drizzle providing some additional accumulations and impacts. The system finally pulled away entirely by the late afternoon and evening of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light wintry mixed precipitation fell, with snow and sleet on the 18th transitioning over to light freezing rain and drizzle during the night, with some additional light snow on the 19th. A storm total snowfall report of 3.5 inches was received from Pennsville, and observations from surrounding areas suggest up to a tenth of an inch of glaze ice likely occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved slowly northeastward over the waters off Delaware and New Jersey on February 1-2, 2021.  The brisk onshore flow resulted in two consecutive high tide cycles with widespread moderate flooding along the New Jersey coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate coastal flooding occurred in the tidal communities of Monmouth County during the night of February 1.  There were numerous road closures.  The water level reached 8.24 feet MLLW at Keansburg, 8.08 feet MLLW at Sandy Hook, 7.26 feet MLLW at Sea Bright, 7.48 feet MLLW at Belmar, and 7.06 feet MLLW at Manasquan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving wave of low pressure tracked northeastward off the Atlantic coast on February 7. The low steadily intensified as it gained latitude, and a sizeable shield of banded precipitation developed in its northwest quadrant. This resulted in a fairly short duration period of precipitation over the eastern mid-Atlantic, but with precipitation rates that were moderate to heavy at times. The storm produced a wide swath of several inches of snow, with considerable local variations in the snow totals. The variations were due to both distinct banding features as well as marginally cold temperatures especially near the coast, which caused precipitation to start as rain in some areas. The storm departed the region by the late afternoon of February 7 after producing a general 4 to 8 inches of snow, with some localized totals that were a little higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the county. A trained spotter just east of Minotola reported 4.4 inches of snow, and a COOP observer in Hammonton reported 3.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the mid-Atlantic coast on April 21. Ahead of the front, modest instability developed in a strongly sheared and strongly forced environment. This led to the development of convection along and ahead of the front as it moved through during the mid afternoon hours. Mixed modes of linear and discrete storm cells were present, some of which became strong to severe. Scattered instances of hail and wind damage were reported across much of the eastern mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by Fortescue NJ Weathernet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over far northern Pennsylvania in an unseasonably warm and unstable air mass south of a stationary front on April 28. This line became a spatially small but long lived bowing segment which produced a path of damaging wind over northern and northeast Pennsylvania. The line then crossed into northern New Jersey, where it gradually weakened due to declining instability near sunset, but still produced some damaging wind in the far northern portion of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some trees were downed in the vicinity of Opsal Ln in the southern portion of Wantage Twp. Power outages were reported. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed near and north of the Baltimore, MD area in an environment of moderate instability and wind shear ahead of a cold front late on April 29. Cloud cover and showers from earlier in the day limited instability for most of the day, but some late day heating allowed sufficient destabilization for a few cells to develop. Storms organized into a line segment and tracked east-northeast into southeast Pennsylvania and south-central New Jersey. The storms became severe for a time, including while passing in and around the Philadelphia metro area, before gradually weakening as they tracked into lesser instability further east. A few reports of damaging wind were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of downed trees and wires in the Cherry Hill area. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a more than two week stretch of dry weather, a cold front approached the mid-Atlantic on May 26. Ahead of the front, an unseasonably hot and humid air mass developed, with high temperatures near to above 90 degrees in much of the region. This contributed to strong instability, while wind shear was more moderate. Multiple rounds of convection affected the region, triggered by a pre-frontal trough and a couple of upper level impulses ahead of the front. A fairly widespread severe weather event resulted, with damaging winds by far the most widespread hazard. Numerous instances of downed trees and power lines and some property damage were reported. Thunderstorms during the evening hours were also prolific lightning producers. The final round of storms finally moved offshore close to midnight after several hours of severe weather in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in Palmyra.

An unseasonably strong weather system affected the mid-Atlantic on April 30, 2021. A cold front moved through the region in the predawn hours of April 30, associated with low pressure moving into coastal New England. An upper level disturbance and a secondary cold front then approached late in the day on the 30th. This system interacted with the departing low pressure over New England, causing that low to rapidly intensify as it began moving towards the Canadian maritimes. With high pressure over the Midwest, this led to a very strong pressure gradient over the mid-Atlantic. Combined with strong dynamics associated with the secondary cold front, a lengthy period of windy conditions developed for much of the day on the 30th. Widespread wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph, with some gusts of 60 to 65 mph, occurred. This, combined with many trees across the region already bearing leaves, led to numerous instances of tree damage and power outages across the region. Scattered showers associated with the secondary front also helped to locally enhance wind damage. Winds finally began to decrease by the night of the 30th as the gradient gradually weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of downed trees and wires were received, including on Woodchurch Rd, US-202, and NJ-29.
